enrichment.short_description: Gravitee is an open-source-rooted API, event and AI agent management platform that lets enterprises design, secure, govern and monetize APIs and event streams from one gateway.
enrichment.pricing_model: Freemium (open-source Community Edition plus tiered commercial plans)
enrichment.seo_title: Gravitee Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Gravitee review covering API gateway and event management features, Community vs Enterprise pricing, AI agent tooling, pros, cons and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Gravitee is an API management and, increasingly, an AI agent management platform that grew out of an open-source project started by a small team of French developers who were frustrated that no single open-source stack covered the full API lifecycle, including a gateway, a developer portal and analytics. The company was founded in 2015 in Lille, France by CEO Rory Blundell together with developers Azize Elamrani, David Brassely, Nicolas Geraud and Titouan Compiegne. The founding team built and released Gravitee as Apache 2.0 licensed open source software first, then layered a commercial Enterprise Edition and hosted services on top once the community edition had proven itself with real adoption, a pattern common to open-core infrastructure companies such as Elastic and Kong. Over the following decade Gravitee relocated its formal headquarters to Denver, Colorado in the United States while keeping a significant engineering base in Lille and a European commercial office in London, reflecting its dual identity as a European-engineered product with a US-facing go-to-market motion. The company has raised meaningful outside capital to fund this expansion, including a $60 million Series C round announced in May 2025, bringing total disclosed funding to roughly $127 million from investors backing its push into event-native APIs and AI infrastructure. Today Gravitee employs a team in the low hundreds spread across its Denver, Lille and London offices. Architecturally, Gravitee is built around a lightweight, high-performance API Gateway that can front REST, GraphQL, gRPC, WebSocket and Kafka-based event traffic, paired with an Access Management module for OAuth2 and OpenID Connect identity flows, an API Designer for building and mocking APIs, an Alert Engine for real-time operational alerting, and Cockpit, a control plane for multi-gateway, multi-environment governance. Because the gateway is protocol-agnostic and can be deployed at the edge, in Kubernetes, or as a managed cloud service, Gravitee is positioned as a single control point across REST APIs, event brokers such as Kafka and Solace, and, in its newest product direction, AI agents and large language model traffic. The company's 2025 and 2026 roadmap has focused heavily on this AI agent management layer, adding an LLM proxy, an MCP (Model Context Protocol) proxy and an agent catalog so that organizations can apply the same governance, rate limiting, observability and security controls to AI agents and MCP servers that they already apply to conventional APIs, plus an Enterprise Auth add-on built on the OpenFGA permission engine. Commercially, Gravitee splits its offering into two parallel product lines, each licensed by the number of production gateways rather than by API call volume, which the company markets as unlimited API calls and events for one predictable monthly price. The API Management line runs Planet at $2,500 per month for a single production gateway with unlimited users and APIs, scaling through the custom-priced Galaxy tier (two gateways plus event broker support and enterprise plugins) up to Universe, a fully custom enterprise tier with four or more gateways, 24/7 support and a dedicated technical account manager. A parallel Event Management line, aimed at teams standardizing on Kafka-style event brokers, starts at Comet for $1,250 per month and scales through Meteor to Asteroid. Every paid tier includes free setup, onboarding and implementation with no separately billed usage fees, and the underlying Community Edition remains free and self-hostable under an Apache 2.0 license for teams that want the open-source gateway without commercial support. Gravitee's target buyer is a platform engineering, integration or API product team inside a mid-market to large enterprise, particularly in regulated industries like banking, insurance, telecom and healthcare where its European origin and self-hosting options resonate with data residency requirements. Its closest competitors are Kong, Apigee (Google), MuleSoft (Salesforce), WSO2 and Tyk, and Gravitee differentiates primarily on its open-source lineage and generous free community tier, its native support for event-driven and streaming protocols alongside REST, and its early, dedicated tooling for governing AI agent and MCP traffic rather than treating it as an afterthought bolted onto a legacy gateway.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Gravitee began life in 2015 as an open-source API gateway built by a small team of developers in Lille, France who wanted a complete, freely available alternative to the expensive proprietary API management suites of the time.

A decade later the company has grown into a full platform spanning API management, event broker mediation, and, most recently, AI agent and MCP protocol governance, while keeping its original open-source Community Edition free and Apache 2.0 licensed.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: At the core of Gravitee is a protocol-agnostic API Gateway that can front REST, GraphQL, gRPC and event-streaming traffic from a single deployment, alongside an Access Management module for OAuth2 and OIDC identity flows.

Newer additions include an LLM proxy and MCP proxy for governing AI agent traffic with the same policies, rate limits and observability applied to conventional APIs, plus Cockpit, a central control plane for managing multiple gateways and environments.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Gravitee prices its API Management line by number of production gateways rather than call volume, starting at Planet for $2,500 per month with unlimited users, APIs and calls on a single gateway, and scaling to custom-priced Galaxy and Universe tiers for larger deployments.

A parallel Event Management line for Kafka-style workloads starts at Comet for $1,250 per month, and the underlying Community Edition remains free and self-hostable for teams that only need the open-source gateway.

features.title: Multi-protocol API Gateway
features.description: A single gateway handles REST, GraphQL, gRPC, WebSocket and Kafka-based event traffic instead of requiring separate tools per protocol.

features.title: Access Management
features.description: Built-in OAuth2 and OpenID Connect identity and access management for securing APIs and consumers.

features.title: API Designer and Developer Portal
features.description: Tools for designing, mocking and publishing APIs to a self-service developer portal for internal and external consumers.

features.title: Cockpit control plane
features.description: Centralized visibility and governance across multiple gateways, environments and teams.

features.title: Alert Engine
features.description: Real-time alerting on gateway health, traffic anomalies and policy violations.

features.title: Event broker mediation
features.description: Native support for Kafka and other event brokers so event-driven architectures get the same governance as REST APIs.

features.title: AI agent and MCP management
features.description: An LLM proxy, MCP proxy and agent catalog that extend API-style governance, rate limiting and observability to AI agents and Model Context Protocol servers.

features.title: Open-source Community Edition
features.description: A free, Apache 2.0 licensed self-hosted edition that includes the core gateway, portal and analytics.

pros: Free, actively maintained open-source Community Edition with no artificial feature caps on core gateway functionality
Unlimited API calls and events per gateway rather than metered usage pricing, which makes costs predictable at scale
Native support for both REST/GraphQL APIs and Kafka-style event streaming in one platform
Early, dedicated tooling for governing AI agents and MCP servers rather than bolting AI support onto a legacy product
cons: Commercial tiers above the entry Planet plan require a custom quote, making upfront cost comparison difficult
Gateway-based pricing can get expensive quickly for organizations that need many production gateways for isolation
Smaller ecosystem and third-party plugin marketplace compared to larger incumbents like Kong or Apigee
Self-hosting the Community Edition requires in-house DevOps expertise for scaling and upgrades

faqs.question: Is Gravitee free to use
faqs.answer: Yes. The Gravitee Community Edition is free and open source under an Apache 2.0 license and can be self-hosted, though it lacks the enterprise support, advanced plugins and managed hosting of the paid tiers.

faqs.question: What does Gravitee cost
faqs.answer: Paid API Management plans start at Planet for 2,500 US dollars per month for one production gateway with unlimited users and API calls, with the Galaxy and Universe tiers priced individually based on gateway count and enterprise requirements.

faqs.question: Does Gravitee support Kafka and event-driven architectures
faqs.answer: Yes. Gravitee has a dedicated Event Management product line, starting at the Comet plan, that mediates Kafka and other event brokers with the same governance model used for REST APIs.

faqs.question: Can Gravitee manage AI agents and MCP servers
faqs.answer: Yes. Gravitee added an LLM proxy, an MCP proxy and an agent catalog so teams can apply rate limiting, security and observability policies to AI agent and Model Context Protocol traffic.

faqs.question: Where is Gravitee headquartered
faqs.answer: Gravitee is headquartered in Denver, Colorado, with additional engineering and commercial offices in Lille, France and London, United Kingdom.

faqs.question: Who are Gravitee's main competitors
faqs.answer: Gravitee competes most directly with Kong, Google Apigee, Salesforce MuleSoft, WSO2 and Tyk in the API management space.

use_cases.title: Enterprise API governance
use_cases.description: Centralizing design, security and monitoring for hundreds of internal and partner-facing APIs behind a consistent gateway and access policy.
use_cases.audience: Platform and integration engineering teams at mid-market to large enterprises

use_cases.description: Applying access control, schema governance and monitoring to Kafka topics and event streams alongside conventional REST APIs.
use_cases.audience: Streaming and event-driven architecture teams

use_cases.title: AI agent and MCP governance
use_cases.description: Applying rate limits, authentication and observability to AI agents and Model Context Protocol servers as they connect to internal systems.

enrichment.short_description: Graylog is an open-source-rooted log management and SIEM platform that helps IT and security teams centralize, search and analyze log data for troubleshooting and threat detection.
enrichment.pricing_model: Open core (free source-available edition plus enterprise subscriptions)
enrichment.seo_title: Graylog Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Graylog review covering log management and SIEM features, Open vs Enterprise vs Security pricing, architecture, pros, cons and alternatives for 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Graylog is a log management and security information and event management (SIEM) platform whose roots trace back to a single frustrated developer rather than a venture-backed startup plan. In February 2009, security-minded software engineer Lennart Koopmann, then working at the German social network XING in Hamburg, began writing what became Graylog2 as a side project after receiving a quote for a one-year commercial log management license so expensive that building his own tool seemed like the more practical option. The early project, sometimes referred to by the codename Torch, was released as free, community-oriented open source software specifically because no viable open-source log analysis platform existed at the time. In November 2012, Hass Chapman joined Koopmann as co-founder to help turn the growing open-source community into a real company, and the pair adopted an open-core business model: a powerful, genuinely useful free tier to build adoption, with enterprise-only features reserved to fund ongoing development. Graylog has since relocated its corporate headquarters to Houston, Texas, while retaining strong roots in its German open-source community, and Koopmann has continued to lead product as the company's Chief Product Officer alongside a separately hired CTO. On the financing side, Graylog has raised capital across several rounds; a widely reported October 2023 raise combined roughly 9 million dollars in new equity with a 30 million dollar debt facility, part of total disclosed funding that different trackers place between roughly 27 million and 65 million dollars depending on which rounds and instruments are counted. As of 2026 the company employs roughly 100 to 135 people. Technically, Graylog centralizes log and event data from servers, network devices, applications and security tools into a searchable, indexed data store built on Elasticsearch/OpenSearch, with a purpose-built query language, dashboards, alerting rules and role-based access control layered on top. The platform is explicitly positioned for lean IT and security teams that need enterprise-grade visibility without the cost or complexity of legacy SIEM vendors like Splunk or IBM QRadar, emphasizing faster time-to-value, lower total cost per gigabyte ingested, and simpler day-to-day operation. Graylog's product line is split into three tiers that share the same core ingestion and search engine. Graylog Open is free and source-available, providing self-service log collection, storage, search and basic analysis with community support, and is commonly used by smaller teams or as an entry point before upgrading. Graylog Enterprise, priced from roughly 15,000 dollars per year and billed by daily data volume or annual consumption, adds features aimed at IT operations and DevOps teams such as archiving, advanced alerting, audit logging, and higher-availability configurations designed to maximize uptime and meet data retention requirements. Graylog Security, priced from roughly 18,000 dollars per year on the same volume-based billing model, layers a full SIEM experience on top of the core platform, including correlation rules, threat intelligence integration, risk scoring and workflows designed to reduce alert fatigue for security operations center analysts. Because pricing scales with daily ingest volume rather than per-seat, Graylog's total cost is most sensitive to how much log data an organization generates rather than how many analysts use the tool, which tends to make it attractive to data-heavy but headcount-lean IT and security teams. Graylog's primary competitors include Splunk, Elastic Security, IBM QRadar, LogRhythm and the broader open-source ELK/OpenSearch stack it was originally built to simplify, and the company differentiates itself by combining a genuinely free, self-hostable open core with a clear, volume-based upgrade path into commercial SIEM and log management tiers rather than forcing a hard cutover from open source to a completely separate commercial product.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Graylog started in 2009 as an open-source side project by German developer Lennart Koopmann, who built his own log management tool after finding commercial alternatives too expensive, and it grew into a company co-founded with Hass Chapman in 2012.

The platform now spans a free, source-available Open edition and paid Enterprise and Security tiers built on the same log ingestion and search engine, serving both IT operations and security operations use cases.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Graylog centralizes logs from servers, applications, network devices and security tools into a searchable, indexed store, with dashboards, custom queries and alerting built on top.
The Security tier adds SIEM capabilities such as correlation rules, threat intelligence integration and risk scoring aimed at reducing alert fatigue for lean security teams.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Graylog Open is free and source-available for teams that want self-managed log collection and search without a subscription.

Graylog Enterprise starts at roughly 15,000 dollars per year and Graylog Security starts at roughly 18,000 dollars per year, both billed based on daily data volume or annual consumption rather than per user.

features.title: Centralized log ingestion
features.description: Collects and normalizes logs from servers, applications, network devices and security tools into one searchable index.

features.title: Custom search and dashboards
features.description: A purpose-built query language and configurable dashboards for exploring log data and building operational views.

features.title: Alerting engine
features.description: Rule-based alerting that notifies teams of anomalies, outages or security events as they happen.

features.title: SIEM correlation and threat intelligence
features.description: In the Security tier, correlation rules and threat intelligence feeds help detect and prioritize genuine security incidents.

features.title: Role-based access control
features.description: Granular permissions so different teams can access only the log streams and dashboards relevant to them.

features.title: Volume-based licensing
features.description: Enterprise and Security plans are priced by daily ingest volume, aligning cost with actual data usage rather than seat count.

features.title: Archiving and retention controls
features.description: Configurable data retention and archiving to meet compliance and audit requirements.

features.title: Open, source-available core
features.description: A free edition built on the same core engine as the paid tiers, allowing teams to start without a licensing commitment.

pros: Genuinely capable free Open edition rather than a crippled trial version
Volume-based pricing can be cost-effective for data-heavy but analyst-lean teams
Built on familiar, widely understood search technology (Elasticsearch/OpenSearch)
Clear upgrade path from free log management into full SIEM without switching platforms
cons: Enterprise and Security pricing is not published and requires a sales quote
Self-managing the Open edition at scale requires real infrastructure and Elasticsearch/OpenSearch expertise
Smaller analyst and integration ecosystem than incumbents like Splunk or QRadar
Volume-based pricing can become expensive quickly for organizations with high daily log volume

faqs.question: Is Graylog free to use
faqs.answer: Yes. Graylog Open is a free, source-available edition that provides log collection, storage, search and basic analysis, though it lacks the advanced alerting, SIEM and support features of the paid tiers.

faqs.question: How much does Graylog Enterprise cost
faqs.answer: Graylog Enterprise starts at roughly 15,000 US dollars per year, billed based on daily data volume or annual consumption rather than per user.

faqs.question: Is Graylog a SIEM tool
faqs.answer: Graylog Security is Graylog's dedicated SIEM product, adding correlation, threat intelligence and risk scoring on top of the core log management platform used by Graylog Open and Enterprise.

faqs.question: Who founded Graylog
faqs.answer: Graylog was founded by Lennart Koopmann, who started the open-source Graylog2 project in 2009, with Hass Chapman joining as co-founder in 2012 to help build the company.

faqs.question: What is Graylog built on
faqs.answer: Graylog's search and storage layer is built on Elasticsearch and OpenSearch, with a custom query language, dashboards and alerting layered on top.

faqs.question: Who competes with Graylog
faqs.answer: Graylog's main competitors include Splunk, Elastic Security, IBM QRadar, LogRhythm and the open-source ELK/OpenSearch stack.

use_cases.title: IT operations troubleshooting
use_cases.description: Centralizing application and infrastructure logs so IT teams can quickly diagnose outages and performance issues.
use_cases.audience: IT operations and DevOps teams

use_cases.title: Security monitoring and threat detection
use_cases.description: Using correlation rules and threat intelligence in Graylog Security to detect and triage security incidents with a lean SOC team.
use_cases.audience: Security operations center analysts

use_cases.title: Compliance log retention
use_cases.description: Meeting regulatory data retention and audit requirements with configurable archiving and access controls.
use_cases.audience: Compliance and risk teams at regulated companies

features: Designed for IT operations and DevOps teams
Risk scoring and alert fatigue reduction

enrichment.short_description: Greenbone is a German vulnerability management company behind OpenVAS, offering GDPR-compliant scanning appliances and software to find and prioritize IT security weaknesses.
enrichment.pricing_model: Open source plus commercial appliances and subscriptions (quote-based)
enrichment.starting_price: Free (OpenVAS/GVM open source); paid appliances via quote, free 14-day trial available
enrichment.seo_title: Greenbone Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Greenbone review covering OpenVAS vulnerability scanning, appliance and cloud pricing, GDPR compliance, key features, pros, cons and alternatives for 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Greenbone is a German cybersecurity company built around OpenVAS, one of the longest-running open-source vulnerability scanning projects in the industry. The company, originally founded as Greenbone Networks in late 2008 in Osnabruck, Germany, took over leadership of the OpenVAS project (a fork of the earlier Nessus scanner after Nessus went closed-source) and progressively turned it from a standalone scanner into a full vulnerability management solution while deliberately keeping its free-software values intact. In 2023 the company restructured into Greenbone AG, a German stock corporation, reflecting its maturation from a scrappy open-source shop into an established security vendor with roughly 15 years of vulnerability research behind it. Greenbone has grown into what it now calls the Greenbone Group, with wholly owned subsidiaries in the United Kingdom, Italy and the Netherlands extending its reach beyond the German-speaking market, and the company reports that its technology protects more than 100,000 installations across over 150 countries, spanning private enterprises, healthcare providers and government agencies. As of the mid-2020s Greenbone employs roughly 115 to 120 people across offices on multiple continents. Notably, Greenbone has built a close relationship with Germany's Federal Office for Information Security (BSI), which has historically supported and referenced Greenbone's tooling, lending the company particular credibility in the German and broader European public sector, and it holds ISO 9001, ISO 27001 and ISO 14001 certifications that matter for enterprise and government procurement. Technically, Greenbone's core engine remains the open-source Greenbone Vulnerability Management (GVM) framework, historically branded OpenVAS, which runs more than 200,000 individual vulnerability tests (VTs) against target systems to identify missing patches, misconfigurations and known CVEs, and the company markets its detection rate as best-in-class. The product line is organized in tiers of increasing sophistication. OpenVAS Basic is the entry-level offering aimed at small companies and individual users, designed to be installed and run quickly with minimal configuration and offered with a free 14-day trial. OpenVAS Scan is the flagship product for companies and public institutions, available as both hardware and virtual appliances so organizations can choose on-premises or virtualized deployment depending on their infrastructure and data residency needs, and it forms the backbone of most commercial Greenbone deployments. Building on that, Greenbone has been rolling out OpenVAS Security Intelligence, a centralized management layer for organizations running many distributed OpenVAS Scan instances that also ingests CSAF security advisories and software bill of materials (SBOM) data to give a consolidated risk picture, and OpenVAS AI, an on-premises artificial intelligence capability designed to turn raw scan output into prioritized remediation action plans without any scan data leaving the customer's network, a deliberate design choice aligned with Greenbone's GDPR-first positioning. Pricing for Greenbone's commercial appliances and subscriptions is not published; prospective customers submit a product request and receive a custom quote based on the number of scanned assets, appliance model and support level required, though the underlying scan engine and vulnerability feed are also available in a community edition that can be self-hosted for free, consistent with the project's open-source origins. Greenbone's target customer ranges from small and mid-sized businesses buying an entry-level appliance to secure a limited environment, up to large enterprises, critical infrastructure operators and public-sector agencies in Europe that specifically value GDPR compliance and the ability to keep vulnerability data entirely within their own jurisdiction rather than sending it to a US-based cloud vendor. Its main competitors include Tenable (Nessus), Qualys, Rapid7 InsightVM and Microsoft Defender Vulnerability Management, and Greenbone differentiates primarily through its open-source heritage and community edition, its strict GDPR and data-residency posture, its long-standing relationship with German federal security authorities, and its emphasis on on-premises deployment options for organizations unwilling or unable to send scan data to a third-party cloud.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Greenbone was founded in 2008 in Osnabruck, Germany to lead development of OpenVAS, an open-source fork of the Nessus vulnerability scanner, and has since grown that project into a full vulnerability management platform sold as appliances and software.

The company converted to a German stock corporation (Greenbone AG) in 2023 and now operates as the Greenbone Group with subsidiaries in the UK, Italy and the Netherlands, reporting more than 100,000 protected installations across over 150 countries.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Greenbone's core scan engine runs more than 200,000 vulnerability tests to identify missing patches, misconfigurations and known CVEs across an organization's IT assets.

Its product line spans an entry-level OpenVAS Basic tier, the flagship OpenVAS Scan appliance available in hardware or virtual form, and newer centralized management and on-premises AI prioritization capabilities.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Greenbone does not publish list prices for its commercial appliances; customers submit a product request and receive a custom quote, with a free 14-day trial available for the entry-level OpenVAS Basic tier.
The underlying open-source Greenbone Vulnerability Management (GVM) engine, historically branded OpenVAS, remains free to self-host for organizations that only need the core scanning capability.

features.title: 200,000+ vulnerability tests
features.description: A continuously updated feed of vulnerability tests covering known CVEs, missing patches and common misconfigurations.

features.title: Hardware and virtual appliances
features.description: OpenVAS Scan is available as a physical appliance or a virtual machine, supporting flexible on-premises deployment.

features.title: GDPR-compliant architecture
features.description: Scan data can be kept entirely within the customer's own infrastructure, aligning with strict European data protection requirements.

features.title: Centralized multi-scanner management
features.description: OpenVAS Security Intelligence consolidates reporting from multiple distributed scan instances into one risk view.

features.title: CSAF and SBOM integration
features.description: Ingests CSAF security advisories and software bill of materials data for a more complete vulnerability and supply-chain picture.

features.title: On-premises AI prioritization
features.description: OpenVAS AI turns raw scan results into prioritized remediation plans without sending data outside the customer's network.

features.title: Open-source community edition
features.description: The core GVM/OpenVAS engine is free and open source, allowing self-hosted use without a commercial license.

features.title: ISO-certified operations
features.description: ISO 9001, ISO 27001 and ISO 14001 certifications support procurement in regulated and public-sector environments.

pros: Free, actively maintained open-source scan engine with a long track record dating back to 2008
Strong GDPR and data-residency positioning, with on-premises deployment options for sensitive environments
Close historical ties to Germany's BSI federal security authority lend added credibility
Flexible deployment via hardware appliance, virtual appliance, or self-hosted open source
cons: Commercial pricing is not published and requires going through a sales quote process
Hardware appliance procurement can be slower and less flexible than pure cloud-based competitors
Smaller global brand recognition than Tenable, Qualys or Rapid7 outside Europe
Newer AI and centralized intelligence features are still rolling out and less mature than the core scanner

faqs.question: Is Greenbone the same as OpenVAS
faqs.answer: Greenbone is the company that leads development of OpenVAS (now formally the Greenbone Vulnerability Management framework) and sells commercial appliances and services built on top of that open-source engine.

faqs.question: Is Greenbone free to use
faqs.answer: The core open-source scanning engine can be self-hosted for free, and the entry-level OpenVAS Basic commercial tier offers a free 14-day trial, but the full appliance and enterprise product lines are paid and quote-based.

faqs.question: How much does Greenbone cost
faqs.answer: Greenbone does not publish list prices; customers request a quote based on appliance type, number of scanned assets and support level.

faqs.question: Is Greenbone GDPR compliant
faqs.answer: Yes. Greenbone markets its products as fully GDPR-compliant and supports on-premises deployments so vulnerability scan data never has to leave the customer's own infrastructure.

faqs.question: Where is Greenbone based
faqs.answer: Greenbone is headquartered in Osnabruck, Germany, and operates as the Greenbone Group with subsidiaries in the United Kingdom, Italy and the Netherlands.

faqs.question: Who competes with Greenbone
faqs.answer: Greenbone competes with Tenable (Nessus), Qualys, Rapid7 InsightVM and Microsoft Defender Vulnerability Management in the vulnerability management space.

use_cases.title: Enterprise vulnerability scanning
use_cases.description: Running scheduled scans across servers, endpoints and network devices to find and prioritize missing patches and misconfigurations.
use_cases.audience: IT security teams at mid-size and large enterprises

use_cases.title: Public sector and regulated compliance
use_cases.description: Meeting GDPR and government security requirements with on-premises scanning that keeps vulnerability data within national or organizational boundaries.
use_cases.audience: Government agencies and critical infrastructure operators

use_cases.title: Small business entry-level scanning
use_cases.description: Installing OpenVAS Basic to quickly identify and remediate obvious security weaknesses without a large security team.
use_cases.audience: Small businesses and individual IT administrators

features: Entry-level scanning for small companies and individuals
Simple install, scan and act workflow

enrichment.short_description: Greenhouse is an applicant tracking and hiring platform used by employers to structure recruiting, source and manage candidates, and reduce bias throughout the hiring process.
enrichment.headquarters: New York, New York, United States
enrichment.seo_title: Greenhouse Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Greenhouse review covering ATS and recruiting features, Core, Plus and Pro pricing tiers, founding story, funding, pros, cons and top alternatives for 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Greenhouse is an applicant tracking system (ATS) and hiring platform that has become one of the most widely used recruiting technologies among mid-size and large employers, particularly technology companies. It was founded in 2012 by Daniel Chait and Jon Stross, both University of Michigan alumni from the class of 1995, who set out to build a more structured, less bias-prone approach to hiring after experiencing firsthand how ad hoc most companies' interview processes were. The company is headquartered in New York City and grew through a series of venture rounds: a 2.7 million dollar seed round in 2013, a 7.5 million dollar Series A in 2014, a 13.6 million dollar Series B in 2015, a 35 million dollar Series C later in 2015, and a 50 million dollar Series D in 2018, bringing total disclosed venture funding to roughly 110 million dollars across eight rounds from investors including Benchmark and Thrive Capital. The company's ownership structure changed significantly in January 2021, when growth equity firm TPG invested 47 million dollars in new capital at an 820 million dollar valuation and separately spent 450 million dollars purchasing shares from existing shareholders, giving TPG a majority stake in the company; that transaction effectively valued Greenhouse at well north of 1 billion dollars and marked its transition from a venture-backed startup to a private-equity-controlled scale-up. Today Greenhouse employs roughly 900 to 950 people and serves thousands of customers, including a significant share of fast-growing technology companies that adopted it as their default ATS during the 2010s hiring boom. Structurally, Greenhouse is built around three integrated modules: an Applicant Tracking System for managing requisitions, candidates and interview pipelines; a Candidate Relationship Management (CRM) and sourcing layer for proactively building talent pipelines before roles open; and a scheduling and analytics layer that helps recruiting teams measure funnel performance and time-to-hire. A defining product philosophy is Greenhouse's emphasis on structured hiring, meaning standardized interview kits, scorecards and defined evaluation criteria applied consistently across candidates for a given role, which the company positions as both a better candidate experience and a way to reduce unconscious bias in hiring decisions, an idea that has become one of Greenhouse's signature differentiators against more transactional ATS competitors. The product is organized into three named pricing tiers, all sold through custom quotes rather than published list prices because Greenhouse pricing depends on plan selection, hiring volume, organizational complexity and the specific features a company needs. Core is positioned for companies establishing consistent hiring processes for the first time or replacing manual, spreadsheet-based recruiting tools, and includes sourcing and CRM, structured interviews, scheduling, standard reporting, single sign-on and Greenhouse's Real Talent matching technology. Plus builds on Core for companies adding new teams or office locations and looking to drive operational efficiency, layering in AI-powered report filters, a Business Intelligence Connector for exporting data into external analytics tools, sourcing automation, candidate texting and fraud detection to catch fabricated candidate identities or AI-generated interview responses. Pro is aimed at large, multi-region organizations managing significant hiring complexity and governance requirements, adding unlimited CRM events, enterprise-grade data security controls, audit logs, a developer sandbox environment for testing integrations, and enterprise-level Real Talent capabilities. Because none of the three tiers has a published price, every prospective customer goes through a sales-assisted quote process, and there is no self-serve free trial or free plan. Greenhouse's core competitors include Lever, Workday Recruiting, iCIMS, SmartRecruiters and Ashby, and the company differentiates primarily through its structured-hiring methodology, its deep native integration ecosystem (Greenhouse has one of the largest marketplaces of pre-built recruiting technology integrations in the industry), and its long-standing reputation among high-growth technology companies as the default choice when scaling a recruiting organization from dozens to thousands of hires per year.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Greenhouse was founded in 2012 in New York by Daniel Chait and Jon Stross to bring more structure and less bias to corporate hiring, and it grew into one of the most widely adopted applicant tracking systems among technology companies.

In January 2021, growth equity firm TPG took a majority stake in Greenhouse through a combined primary investment and secondary share purchase valuing the company at roughly 820 million dollars, shifting it from a purely venture-backed company to a private-equity-controlled one.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Greenhouse combines an applicant tracking system, a candidate relationship management and sourcing layer, and scheduling and analytics into one platform built around structured, scorecard-based interviewing.
Higher tiers add AI-powered reporting, sourcing automation, candidate texting, fraud detection for fabricated candidates, and enterprise governance features like audit logs and a developer sandbox.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Greenhouse sells three tiers, Core, Plus and Pro, each targeted at a different stage of hiring maturity and organizational complexity, but none publishes a fixed price.
Pricing is customized based on plan, hiring volume and required features, so prospective customers must request a demo or quote from Greenhouse's sales team.

features.title: Structured hiring and scorecards
features.description: Standardized interview kits and scorecards applied consistently across candidates to reduce bias and improve evaluation quality.

features.title: Candidate relationship management (CRM)
features.description: Tools for proactively building and nurturing talent pipelines ahead of open requisitions.

features.title: Scheduling automation
features.description: Automated interview scheduling that coordinates across interviewers, candidates and calendars.

features.title: Reporting and analytics
features.description: Dashboards for tracking recruiting funnel metrics, time-to-hire and diversity of candidate pipelines.

features.title: Sourcing automation
features.description: Automated outreach and pipeline-building tools available on the Plus and Pro tiers.

features.title: Fraud detection
features.description: Tools designed to flag fabricated candidates or AI-generated interview responses.

features.title: Extensive integration marketplace
features.description: Pre-built integrations with background check, assessment, HRIS and job board vendors.

features.title: Enterprise governance controls
features.description: Audit logs, developer sandbox and enterprise data security available on the Pro tier for large, multi-region organizations.

pros: Structured, scorecard-based hiring methodology is well regarded for improving consistency and reducing bias
One of the largest third-party integration marketplaces among applicant tracking systems
Strong brand recognition and reference base among fast-growing technology companies
Three-tier structure lets organizations grow from basic ATS use into full enterprise hiring governance
cons: No published pricing, requiring a sales-assisted quote for even basic evaluation
No self-serve free trial or free plan for smaller teams to test the product
Can be more complex and heavier to configure than lightweight ATS competitors for very small teams
Some advanced features such as fraud detection and BI connectors are locked behind higher-priced tiers

faqs.question: How much does Greenhouse cost
faqs.answer: Greenhouse does not publish fixed prices. Cost is customized based on the Core, Plus or Pro plan selected, hiring volume, organizational complexity and required features, determined through a sales quote.

faqs.question: Who founded Greenhouse
faqs.answer: Greenhouse was founded in 2012 by Daniel Chait and Jon Stross, both University of Michigan graduates, with the goal of making hiring more structured and less biased.

faqs.question: Is Greenhouse owned by TPG
faqs.answer: TPG became Greenhouse's majority owner in January 2021 through a combined 47 million dollar primary investment and a 450 million dollar purchase of shares from existing investors.

faqs.question: What is structured hiring in Greenhouse
faqs.answer: Structured hiring refers to Greenhouse's methodology of standardized interview kits and scorecards applied consistently to every candidate for a role, intended to improve decision quality and reduce bias.

faqs.question: Does Greenhouse offer a free trial
faqs.answer: Greenhouse does not publicly advertise a self-serve free trial or free plan; prospective customers go through a demo and custom quote process instead.

faqs.question: Who are Greenhouse's main competitors
faqs.answer: Greenhouse's main competitors include Lever, Workday Recruiting, iCIMS, SmartRecruiters and Ashby.

use_cases.title: Scaling technology company hiring
use_cases.description: Standardizing recruiting workflows and interview processes as a company scales from dozens to thousands of annual hires.
use_cases.audience: Talent acquisition teams at high-growth technology companies

use_cases.title: Reducing hiring bias
use_cases.description: Using structured scorecards and standardized interview kits to apply consistent evaluation criteria across all candidates for a role.
use_cases.audience: HR and DEI-focused hiring teams

use_cases.title: Enterprise multi-region recruiting governance
use_cases.description: Managing complex, multi-country hiring with audit logs, enterprise security and a developer sandbox for custom integrations.
use_cases.audience: Enterprise HR and IT teams at large, multi-region organizations

enrichment.short_description: Grid My Business is an AI-powered local SEO platform that tracks Google Maps geo-grid rankings, manages Google Business Profiles, and automates reviews and posts for local businesses and agencies.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (Starter, 250 scan credits/month); paid plans from $29/month
enrichment.seo_title: Grid My Business Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Grid My Business review covering geo-grid rank tracking, Google Business Profile management, AI automation, Growth, Scale and Dominate pricing for 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Grid My Business is a local search engine optimization platform focused on one specific, high-value problem for local businesses and marketing agencies: understanding and improving how a business ranks on Google Maps and in the local map pack across the different neighborhoods, zip codes and streets that make up its service area. The product launched in October 2021 as a local rank monitoring tool built around a distinctive visual geo-grid interface, which plots a business's Google Maps ranking as a grid of pins across a geographic area rather than showing a single average rank, making it immediately visible where a business is strong and where it is invisible to nearby searchers. That geo-grid approach places Grid My Business in the same product category as tools like Local Falcon and Places Scout, which pioneered grid-based local rank tracking as an alternative to traditional single-point keyword rank trackers that don't capture how dramatically local rankings can vary by physical location. Since its 2021 launch, the product has expanded well beyond pure rank tracking into a fuller local SEO and Google Business Profile (GBP) management suite, adding continuous ranking monitoring on an automated cadence of roughly every three days, competitor tracking that benchmarks a business against nearby rivals, and a GMB Score feature that synthesizes rankings, profile completeness and review signals into a single health metric. On the Google Business Profile side, the platform now offers direct GBP integration for editing and managing listings, an AI Business Protector that alerts owners to unauthorized or suspicious edits to their profile, a citation manager that checks and helps correct business listings across more than 1,000 online directories, and AI-driven performance insights that recommend specific optimization actions. A significant part of the product's recent growth has come from customer engagement automation: AI agents that draft and post automated responses to customer reviews, an AI-powered post scheduler that keeps a Google Business Profile active with regular updates, and a broader media scheduler for managing content across profiles. The platform also added a performance metrics capability that pulls customer engagement and conversion data directly into its dashboard and client-facing Business Profile Reports, and it has released an MCP (Model Context Protocol) server that gives AI assistants direct, read-only access to a customer's account data so that local SEO metrics can be pulled by AI tools without manual exports, reflecting the product's positioning as an AI-forward entrant in a historically manual, spreadsheet-heavy local SEO niche. Grid My Business is explicitly built to serve two distinct audiences from one account structure: individual local business owners managing their own single location, and marketing agencies managing dozens or hundreds of client locations, the latter supported by multi-location management with bulk actions, separate organized workspaces per client, and white-label reporting that lets agencies present Grid My Business data under their own branding. The product also has built-in support for Service Area Businesses (SABs), companies like plumbers, electricians or cleaning services that don't have a single storefront but serve a defined geographic radius, a segment that many local SEO tools handle poorly. Pricing follows a straightforward freemium SaaS model available in over 195 countries with a 30-day money-back guarantee on paid plans. A free Starter tier provides 250 scan credits per month and covers two business profiles, enough for a single small business owner to get a basic sense of their local visibility. Paid plans scale by both scan volume and account limits: Growth is priced at 29 dollars per month, or 24 dollars per month when billed annually at 290 dollars per year, and includes roughly 300 scans per month across up to 100 business profiles and 200 tracked competitors; Scale is priced at 69 dollars per month, or 58 dollars per month billed annually at 690 dollars per year, with roughly 800 scans per month across up to 300 profiles and 600 competitors; and Dominate, the top consumer-facing tier, is priced at 89 dollars per month, or 74 dollars per month billed annually at 890 dollars per year, offering roughly 1,200 scans per month, unlimited business profiles and up to 1,000 tracked competitors, making it the natural choice for agencies managing many client locations. Grid My Business competes most directly with Local Falcon, Places Scout, BrightLocal and Whitespark in the geo-grid and local SEO reporting category, and it differentiates primarily through its combination of frequent automated grid scanning, deep native Google Business Profile management and AI-driven review and post automation, and its recent push into AI-assistant connectivity via its MCP server, positioning it as a more automation-heavy alternative to reporting-only local rank trackers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Grid My Business launched in October 2021 as a visual geo-grid rank tracker that shows how a business ranks on Google Maps across many nearby locations at once, rather than a single average ranking.

The product has since grown into a fuller local SEO suite covering Google Business Profile management, review and post automation, citation management, and reporting for both individual businesses and agencies.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Core functionality centers on automated geo-grid scans that run roughly every three days, competitor tracking, and a GMB Score that combines rankings, profile completeness and review signals into one metric.

Additional tools include AI-powered review response automation, a post scheduler, a citation manager covering over 1,000 directories, and an MCP server that gives AI assistants direct access to account data.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: A free Starter tier includes 250 scan credits per month and two business profiles, letting individual business owners try the platform at no cost.

Paid plans, Growth, Scale and Dominate, start at 29 dollars per month and scale up to 89 dollars per month based on scan volume, tracked profiles and competitors, with a 20 to 25 percent discount for annual billing.

features.description: Direct GBP integration for editing listings, with AI-driven alerts on unauthorized profile changes.

features.description: AI agents that draft and post responses to customer reviews automatically.

features.description: Checks and helps correct business listing consistency across more than 1,000 online directories.

features.title: Competitor tracking
features.description: Benchmarks a business's local rankings against nearby competitors.

features.title: White-label agency reporting
features.description: Multi-location management with bulk actions and branded reports for agencies managing many client accounts.

features.title: MCP server for AI assistants
features.description: Gives AI tools direct, read-only access to local SEO account data without manual exports.

pros: Genuinely free Starter tier rather than a time-limited trial
Visual geo-grid format makes uneven local rankings easy to spot at a glance
Strong native Google Business Profile management alongside rank tracking, not just reporting
Built-in support for Service Area Businesses, which many local SEO tools handle poorly
cons: Newer entrant to the geo-grid category with less brand history than Local Falcon or BrightLocal
Higher-volume scanning and competitor tracking are locked behind the Scale and Dominate tiers
Publicly available company background information (founders, headquarters) is limited
Scan credit limits on lower tiers can be restrictive for agencies with many client locations

faqs.question: Is Grid My Business free
faqs.answer: Yes. A free Starter tier includes 250 scan credits per month and covers two business profiles, with paid Growth, Scale and Dominate plans available for higher volume needs.

faqs.question: What is geo-grid rank tracking
faqs.answer: Geo-grid rank tracking shows how a business ranks on Google Maps across a grid of nearby locations, revealing where visibility is strong or weak rather than a single average ranking figure.

faqs.question: Does Grid My Business support agencies
faqs.answer: Yes. Grid My Business supports multi-location management, bulk actions and white-label reporting designed for agencies managing many client Google Business Profiles.

faqs.question: Does Grid My Business work for service area businesses
faqs.answer: Yes. The platform has built-in support for Service Area Businesses that serve a geographic radius rather than operating from a single storefront.

faqs.question: How much does Grid My Business cost
faqs.answer: Paid plans start at 29 dollars per month for Growth and go up to 89 dollars per month for Dominate, with annual billing offering a discount, alongside a free Starter tier.

faqs.question: What is the Grid My Business MCP server
faqs.answer: It is a Model Context Protocol server that gives AI assistants direct, read-only access to a customer's local SEO account data so metrics can be pulled without manual exports.

enrichment.short_description: Grip is an AI-powered event networking and matchmaking platform that helps conference and trade show organizers connect attendees, exhibitors and sponsors through intelligent meeting recommendations.
enrichment.seo_title: Grip Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Grip review covering AI event matchmaking, mobile event app, lead retrieval, onsite tools, funding history, pros, cons and alternatives for 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Grip is a business-to-business event technology platform built around one core idea: that the most valuable outcome of a conference or trade show is not the sessions but the right people meeting each other, and that meeting the right people can be engineered with data and artificial intelligence rather than left to chance or a printed attendee list. The company was founded in 2014 in London by Brent Hoberman, the well-known British entrepreneur best known for co-founding lastminute.com and later Founders Factory and Founders Forum, together with co-founder Tim Groot. Grip spent its early years as a niche AI matchmaking add-on for events before the COVID-19 pandemic forced a large-scale pivot: when in-person events shut down in 2020, Grip rebuilt itself around virtual and hybrid event support, and that pivot proved commercially significant. In February 2021, Grip announced a 13 million dollar (roughly 10.7 million euro, or 9.3 million pound) Series A funding round led by London-based growth investor Kennet Partners, bringing the company's total disclosed funding to roughly 14.5 million dollars at the time, capital that Grip used to expand its platform beyond matchmaking into a fuller event technology suite as organizers prepared for a return to in-person and hybrid formats. Since then, Grip has continued to grow through both organic product development and acquisition, including acquiring an event management system (EMS) from US-based Connectiv Holdings to extend its platform into full event management rather than networking alone, positioning Grip as an end-to-end alternative to point solutions. The company is based in London with roughly 51 to 200 employees and has built a customer base among some of the largest names in the global events industry, including Money 20/20, Clarion Events, Messe Frankfurt, Reed Exhibitions, VentureBeat and TechCrunch, and it states that its platform now supports more than 5,000 events per year reaching over 15 million participants. Product-wise, Grip is organized as a modular platform spanning engagement, management and onsite capabilities that organizers can mix and match depending on event format and size. Its signature feature is AI-powered matchmaking, which analyzes attendee profile data, stated interests and behavioral signals to automatically generate highly relevant, mutual one-to-one meeting suggestions between attendees, exhibitors and sponsors, replacing the manual spreadsheet-based matchmaking that many large trade shows historically relied on. On top of matchmaking, Grip offers a customizable, white-label mobile event app that functions as the digital hub of an event, providing lead scanning for exhibitors, interactive floor plans and wayfinding, personalized attendee schedules and searchable exhibitor directories. The platform supports more than a dozen distinct meeting formats beyond simple one-to-one meetings, including hosted buyer programs, speed networking sessions, roundtables and dedicated investor meeting formats, reflecting Grip's roots serving large B2B trade shows and investment-focused conferences. For physical events, Grip provides onsite tools including badge printing, entrance scanning, smart badges and location tracking, alongside lead retrieval and sponsorship monetization tools that let exhibitors capture and qualify leads and let organizers sell premium visibility to sponsors. More recently the company has added an AI assistant capability designed to resolve attendee questions instantly during an event while reducing the operational burden on the organizer's support staff. Because Grip sells into large enterprise event organizers, trade show producers and conference companies rather than individual consumers, it does not publish self-serve pricing; deals are negotiated per event or per annual contract based on attendee volume, feature modules selected and the level of onsite hardware and support required. Grip's primary competitors include Bizzabo, Cvent, Swapcard and Brella in the broader event technology and matchmaking space, and it differentiates itself primarily through the depth and accuracy of its AI matchmaking engine, its long list of marquee enterprise trade show and conference customers, and its modular architecture that lets organizers combine engagement, full event management and onsite hardware within one platform rather than stitching together separate vendors.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Grip was founded in London in 2014 by Brent Hoberman, co-founder of lastminute.com, and Tim Groot, built around AI-driven attendee matchmaking for business events.

A pivot to virtual and hybrid events during the COVID-19 pandemic, followed by a 13 million dollar Series A round in February 2021, helped Grip expand into a full event technology platform used by major trade show and conference organizers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Grip's core feature is AI-powered matchmaking that analyzes attendee data to generate relevant one-to-one meeting suggestions between attendees, exhibitors and sponsors.
The platform also includes a white-label mobile event app, more than a dozen meeting formats, onsite badge and lead retrieval tools, and sponsorship monetization capabilities.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Grip does not publish self-serve pricing because it sells to enterprise event organizers, trade show producers and conference companies through negotiated, per-event or annual contracts.
Pricing depends on attendee volume, which product modules (engagement, management, onsite) are selected, and the level of onsite hardware and support required.

features.description: Tools that let organizers package and sell premium visibility and engagement opportunities to sponsors.

Strong roster of marquee enterprise event customers including Money 20/20 and Messe Frankfurt
Modular platform covers engagement, full event management and onsite hardware in one system
Supports a wide range of meeting formats beyond simple one-to-one networking
cons: No published pricing; every deal requires a sales conversation and custom quote
Built primarily for large enterprise events, which may be overkill or unaffordable for small event organizers
Onsite hardware tools add logistical complexity compared to purely software-based competitors
Feature depth can require significant setup and configuration ahead of an event

faqs.question: What does Grip do
faqs.answer: Grip is an AI-powered event technology platform that uses attendee data to generate matchmaking recommendations and provides a mobile event app, onsite tools and sponsorship features for conferences and trade shows.

faqs.question: How much does Grip cost
faqs.answer: Grip does not publish pricing. It sells to enterprise event organizers through custom, per-event or annual contracts based on attendee volume and selected product modules.

faqs.question: Who founded Grip
faqs.answer: Grip was founded in 2014 in London by Brent Hoberman, co-founder of lastminute.com, and Tim Groot.

faqs.question: Does Grip support in-person, virtual and hybrid events
faqs.answer: Yes. Grip expanded from its original in-person matchmaking focus to fully support virtual and hybrid events during the COVID-19 pandemic and continues to support all three formats.

faqs.question: Who uses Grip
faqs.answer: Grip is used by large event organizers and trade show producers including Money 20/20, Clarion Events, Messe Frankfurt and Reed Exhibitions.

faqs.question: Who competes with Grip
faqs.answer: Grip competes with Bizzabo, Cvent, Swapcard and Brella in the event technology and matchmaking space.
