enrichment.short_description: Grist is an open-source, self-hostable spreadsheet with database powers, combining familiar spreadsheet editing with relational data, formulas and a REST API.
enrichment.pricing_model: Freemium (open-source self-hosted plus per-user cloud subscriptions)
enrichment.starting_price: Free (self-hosted or cloud Free plan); paid plans from $10/user/month
enrichment.headquarters: New York, New York, United States
enrichment.seo_title: Grist Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Grist review covering the open-source spreadsheet-database hybrid, self-hosting options, Free, Pro, Business and Enterprise pricing, pros, cons for 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Grist is an open-source spreadsheet-database hybrid built by Grist Labs, a small, deliberately self-funded software company based in New York City. Grist Labs was founded in 2014 by brothers Dmitry and Stan Sagalovskiy; Dmitry had already co-founded Hudson River Trading, a high-frequency trading firm, in 2002, and the New York Math Circle, an educational non-profit, in 2008, giving the founding team both deep technical credibility and a track record of building durable, mission-driven organizations before starting Grist Labs. According to the company's own account, Grist Labs was founded not long after the 2013 Snowden mass-surveillance disclosures revealed how vulnerable even reputable cloud services could be, and the founders concluded that the best remedy available to them was open-source code, transparent security practices, and giving users the option to run their own infrastructure rather than trusting a closed, remote service by default. That founding philosophy shaped Grist's entire product and business strategy: rather than raising venture capital and optimizing for growth at any cost, Grist Labs has remained self-funded throughout its history, and the company later open-sourced Grist itself, releasing the core grist-core engine along with grist-desktop and grist-static under the Apache License, Version 2.0, so that any individual or organization can run a full Grist instance on their own servers with no dependency on Grist Labs' hosted service. As of the mid-2020s Grist Labs remains a small, distributed team of roughly a dozen to several dozen people spread across North America, Europe and Africa, deliberately staying lean relative to the size of its user base. Product-wise, Grist occupies a distinct niche between conventional spreadsheets like Excel or Google Sheets and full relational databases like Airtable or Postgres-backed no-code tools. It looks and feels like a spreadsheet, with a familiar grid interface, formulas and instant editing, but under the hood every Grist document is backed by a real relational data model, meaning tables can be properly linked to each other, data types are enforced, and large datasets stay fast and reliable in ways that traditional spreadsheets struggle with once they grow past a few thousand rows. On top of that data layer, Grist adds features associated with no-code application builders: customizable views and widgets (grid, card, chart and other layouts over the same underlying data), page layouts that can be assembled into lightweight internal tools, granular access control that can restrict permissions down to specific rows, columns or even individual cells, formulas written in real Python rather than a proprietary spreadsheet formula language, a REST API for programmatic access, and, more recently, an AI Assistant that can help users write formulas, clean data or build new views using natural language. Grist's pricing is split cleanly between a self-hosted, fully open-source path and a cloud-hosted, per-user subscription path, and both are actively maintained. The self-hosted Community Edition is completely free and open source, downloadable from GitHub, with community-only support, making it a natural fit for privacy-conscious individuals, nonprofits or organizations with existing infrastructure. The cloud-hosted Free plan is also 0 dollars per user per month and includes up to 5,000 records per document, a lifetime allotment of 100 AI Assistant credits, and support for up to two guests per document. The Pro plan costs 10 dollars per user per month, or 8 dollars per user per month when billed annually (a 20 percent discount), and raises the per-document record limit to 100,000, adds three years of document snapshot history, and increases the AI Assistant allotment to 100 credits per month along with support for unlimited documents. The Business plan costs 30 dollars per user per month, or 24 dollars per user per month billed annually, requires a minimum of five users, and further raises limits to 150,000 records per document, five years of snapshot history, 2,000 AI Assistant credits per month, workflow automations and priority email support. An Enterprise plan, available both cloud-hosted and self-hosted, is custom priced and adds advanced customization, dedicated servers in a chosen region, custom SSO and domain branding, audit log streaming and deeper administrative controls, with self-hosted Enterprise customers additionally getting priority support and access to pre-release security updates. Grist Labs also offers a 50 percent discount on the Pro plan for registered nonprofits, and organizations with less than 1 million dollars in annual revenue can request free activation of paid features. Grist's closest competitors are Airtable, Baserow, NocoDB and, to a lesser extent, Google Sheets and Excel for teams that eventually outgrow a pure spreadsheet, and it differentiates itself primarily through its combination of a fully open-source, self-hostable core, Python-based formulas instead of a proprietary formula language, cell-level access control, and a self-funded, privacy-first company culture that has deliberately avoided the venture-capital-driven growth pressure common among its no-code database competitors.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Grist Labs was founded in 2014 in New York City by brothers Dmitry and Stan Sagalovskiy, who built Grist around the belief that open-source, self-hostable software was the best defense against the surveillance and vendor lock-in risks exposed by the 2013 Snowden disclosures.

The company has remained self-funded throughout its history and has open-sourced Grist's core engine under the Apache 2.0 license, letting anyone run a full Grist instance on their own infrastructure alongside the company's hosted cloud service.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Grist looks like a familiar spreadsheet but is backed by a real relational data model, so tables can be linked together with enforced data types even as documents grow large.

It supports Python-based formulas, multiple views over the same data (grid, card, chart), cell-level access control, a REST API, and an AI Assistant for writing formulas and building views with natural language.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Grist offers both a fully open-source, self-hosted Community Edition and a cloud-hosted Free plan, both at no cost, with the cloud Free plan capped at 5,000 records per document.

Paid cloud plans start at Pro for 10 dollars per user per month (8 dollars billed annually), rising to Business at 30 dollars per user per month, with a custom-priced Enterprise tier available both cloud-hosted and self-hosted.

features.title: Spreadsheet interface with database powers
features.description: A familiar grid editing experience backed by a real relational data model with enforced data types.

features.title: Python-based formulas
features.description: Formulas are written in real Python rather than a proprietary spreadsheet language, enabling more powerful logic.

features.title: Multiple data views
features.description: The same underlying data can be displayed as grid, card, chart or other custom layouts.

features.title: Cell-level access control
features.description: Permissions can be restricted down to specific rows, columns or individual cells.

features.title: REST API
features.description: Programmatic access to documents and data for integrations and automation.

features.title: AI Assistant
features.description: Helps users write formulas, clean data or build new views using natural language prompts.

features.title: Self-hosting via open source
features.description: The core grist-core engine is Apache 2.0 licensed and can be run entirely on a customer's own infrastructure.

features.title: Document snapshot history
features.description: Automatic version history of documents, with retention length varying by plan.

pros: Fully open-source and self-hostable, avoiding vendor lock-in and supporting strict data residency needs
Real relational data model under a familiar spreadsheet interface, more robust than plain spreadsheets at scale
Python-based formulas are more powerful and transparent than typical proprietary spreadsheet formula languages
Self-funded company culture with no venture capital growth pressure, plus nonprofit discounts
cons: Smaller ecosystem of templates and integrations compared to Airtable
Free cloud plan's 5,000 record per document limit is restrictive for larger datasets
Self-hosting requires technical setup and maintenance effort not needed with a fully managed SaaS
AI Assistant credit allotments on lower tiers are limited compared to dedicated AI-first tools

faqs.question: Is Grist free to use
faqs.answer: Yes. Grist offers a free self-hosted Community Edition under an Apache 2.0 open-source license, as well as a free cloud-hosted plan with up to 5,000 records per document.

faqs.question: Can Grist be self-hosted
faqs.answer: Yes. Grist's core engine, grist-core, along with grist-desktop and grist-static, is open source and can be run on a customer's own infrastructure, with a paid self-hosted Enterprise tier available for larger deployments.

faqs.question: How is Grist different from Airtable
faqs.answer: Grist uses a familiar spreadsheet-style grid interface backed by a true relational data model and Python-based formulas, and it offers a fully open-source, self-hostable option, whereas Airtable is a closed, cloud-only platform.

faqs.question: How much does Grist cost
faqs.answer: Grist's cloud plans start free, then Pro is 10 dollars per user per month (8 dollars billed annually), Business is 30 dollars per user per month with a 5-user minimum, and Enterprise is custom priced.

faqs.question: Who founded Grist
faqs.answer: Grist Labs was founded in 2014 in New York City by brothers Dmitry and Stan Sagalovskiy and has remained self-funded without external venture capital.

faqs.question: Does Grist offer nonprofit discounts
faqs.answer: Yes. Registered nonprofits receive a 50 percent discount on the Pro plan, and organizations with under 1 million dollars in annual revenue can request free activation.

use_cases.title: Team database without a developer
use_cases.description: Building linked, relational data tools for project tracking, inventories or CRM-style workflows without writing custom software.
use_cases.audience: Small teams and operations staff without dedicated engineering support

use_cases.title: Privacy-first self-hosted data management
use_cases.description: Running Grist entirely on internal infrastructure to keep sensitive data off third-party cloud servers.
use_cases.audience: Privacy-conscious organizations, nonprofits and government teams

use_cases.title: Replacing fragile spreadsheets
use_cases.description: Migrating large, error-prone Excel or Google Sheets workbooks into a more robust relational structure with enforced data types.
use_cases.audience: Analysts and operations teams outgrowing traditional spreadsheets

enrichment.short_description: Grok is xAI's conversational AI chatbot, available on the web at grok.com, in X, and via iOS and Android apps, with free and paid SuperGrok subscription tiers.
enrichment.pricing_model: Freemium subscription plus usage-based API pricing
enrichment.seo_title: Grok Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Grok review covering xAI's chatbot, Grok 4.5 models, free vs SuperGrok pricing tiers, API costs, features, pros, cons and top AI chatbot alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Grok is the conversational AI chatbot built by xAI, the artificial intelligence company Elon Musk incorporated in Nevada on March 9, 2023 and publicly launched on July 12, 2023 with a team of AI researchers recruited from DeepMind, OpenAI and other leading AI labs, with a stated mission focused on advancing scientific discovery and understanding the universe. Grok itself first shipped in November 2023 as a feature exclusive to X Premium subscribers, tightly integrated into the X (formerly Twitter) platform that Musk also owns, distinguishing it from competitors like ChatGPT and Gemini by giving it real-time access to X's live post feed and a deliberately more irreverent, less filtered conversational personality. The model and product evolved quickly: Grok-1.5 arrived in March 2024 with an expanded 128,000 token context window, Grok-2 launched in beta in August 2024 for Premium and Premium+ subscribers, and in December 2024 xAI made Grok available to free X users for the first time, albeit with usage limits, while simultaneously releasing standalone Grok web and iOS apps in beta, initially limited to Australia. Worldwide availability of the standalone apps followed on January 9, 2025, formally separating Grok from a pure X-Premium perk into an independent product family accessible via grok.com and dedicated mobile apps. Grok 3 launched in February 2025, followed by Grok 4 in July 2025, and xAI added an image and video generation capability called Grok Imagine in July 2025, expanding Grok from a text chatbot into a broader multimodal AI product; Grok 4.5 followed in mid-2026. xAI's financial trajectory has been extraordinarily fast even by AI industry standards: after a 134.7 million dollar seed round in December 2023, the company raised a 6 billion dollar Series B at a 24 billion dollar valuation in May 2024, a 6 billion dollar Series C at a 50 billion dollar valuation in December 2024, roughly 10 billion dollars in equity at a 200 billion dollar valuation in September 2025, and was reportedly targeting a 15 billion dollar raise at a 230 billion dollar pre-money valuation in a Series E round in December 2025, alongside a 5 billion dollar debt facility arranged by Morgan Stanley; total primary funding raised is estimated at over 22 billion dollars, with backers including Sequoia Capital, Andreessen Horowitz and Valor Equity Partners. Ownership structure has shifted twice in dramatic fashion: in March 2025, xAI acquired X in an all-stock transaction that valued xAI at roughly 80 billion dollars and X at roughly 33 billion dollars, folding Musk's social network directly into the AI company, and then in February 2026 SpaceX, also controlled by Musk, acquired xAI, bringing Grok, X and SpaceX under increasingly consolidated ownership. xAI's headquarters is at 1450 Page Mill Road in Palo Alto, California, with an additional office in San Francisco's Mission District (in what was previously OpenAI's own headquarters building) and a large compute buildout centered on the Colossus supercomputer cluster in Memphis, Tennessee. From a product standpoint, Grok is positioned as a general-purpose conversational AI assistant that can answer questions, write and debug code, analyze images, reason through complex problems, and, through Grok Imagine, generate images and video from text prompts, competing directly with OpenAI's ChatGPT, Google's Gemini and Anthropic's Claude. Its most distinctive differentiator remains its deep, native integration with real-time data from X, giving Grok access to live commentary and breaking news other assistants must retrieve through separate search tools, alongside a brand personality that leans into humor and a self-described willingness to answer edgier questions that some competitors decline. Pricing is split across two parallel but overlapping paths as of 2026: a free tier available directly at grok.com or through basic X access offering roughly 10 prompts every two hours on a lighter model; SuperGrok Lite at 10 dollars per month, a lower-cost standalone tier introduced in March 2026 sitting between free and full SuperGrok; SuperGrok at 30 dollars per month or 300 dollars per year, the primary standalone consumer subscription with access to Grok's flagship models; and SuperGrok Heavy at 300 dollars per month, the top consumer tier with guaranteed access to xAI's most capable model at all times. In parallel, X bundles Grok access into its own subscriptions: X Premium at 8 dollars per month includes basic Grok access alongside X platform perks, while X Premium+ at 40 dollars per month bundles fuller Grok access with an ad-free X experience and higher usage throughput. For businesses, xAI offers a Grok Business plan priced at 30 dollars per user per month, and developers can access Grok programmatically through xAI's API, priced per million tokens and varying by model, with the flagship Grok 4.5 priced at roughly 2 dollars per million input tokens (0.50 dollars for cached input) and 6 dollars per million output tokens as of mid-2026. Grok's core competitive set includes ChatGPT, Gemini, Claude and Meta AI, and it differentiates primarily through its real-time X data integration, its unusually rapid model release cadence funded by one of the largest capital raises in AI industry history, and its increasingly tight integration with Musk's other companies, X and SpaceX, following the 2025 and 2026 ownership consolidations.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Grok is the AI chatbot built by xAI, the company Elon Musk founded in March 2023 and launched publicly in July 2023, first shipping to X Premium subscribers in November 2023 before becoming a standalone product at grok.com and via iOS and Android apps worldwide in January 2025.

xAI has raised over 22 billion dollars across a rapid sequence of funding rounds, acquired X in an all-stock deal in March 2025, and was itself acquired by SpaceX in February 2026, consolidating Grok, X and SpaceX under shared Musk-controlled ownership.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Grok functions as a general-purpose conversational assistant for answering questions, writing and debugging code, reasoning through complex problems and analyzing images, with real-time access to live posts and trends from X.

Grok Imagine, launched in July 2025, extended the product into image and video generation from text prompts, and successive model releases (Grok 3, Grok 4, Grok 4.5) have steadily expanded context length and reasoning capability.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Grok offers a free tier with limited prompts, alongside standalone SuperGrok subscriptions: SuperGrok Lite at 10 dollars per month, SuperGrok at 30 dollars per month, and SuperGrok Heavy at 300 dollars per month for guaranteed top-tier model access.

Grok access is also bundled into X Premium (8 dollars per month) and X Premium+ (40 dollars per month), with a Grok Business plan at 30 dollars per user per month and usage-based API pricing for developers.

features.title: Conversational AI assistant
features.description: Answers questions, explains concepts, and assists with writing, coding and reasoning tasks through natural conversation.

features.title: Real-time X data integration
features.description: Native access to live posts and trends on X gives Grok up-to-date awareness of breaking news and public conversation.

features.title: Grok Imagine (image and video generation)
features.description: Generates images and short videos from text prompts, extending Grok beyond a text-only chatbot.

features.title: Multimodal image analysis
features.description: Can analyze and answer questions about uploaded images and screenshots.

features.title: Rapid model iteration
features.description: Frequent flagship model releases (Grok 3, Grok 4, Grok 4.5) with expanding context windows and reasoning ability.

features.title: Cross-platform access
features.description: Available on the web at grok.com, inside X, and via dedicated iOS and Android apps.

features.title: Developer API
features.description: Programmatic access to Grok models priced per million input and output tokens for building custom applications.

features.title: Business plan
features.description: A dedicated per-user Grok Business subscription for organizational deployment.

pros: Real-time integration with X gives Grok access to live public conversation and breaking news other chatbots lack
Backed by extremely large capital raises, funding an unusually fast pace of model releases
Genuinely usable free tier alongside multiple paid tiers for different budgets and usage levels
Available across web, X, iOS and Android with a dedicated business plan for teams
cons: Free tier prompt limits (roughly 10 every two hours) are restrictive compared to some competitors' free tiers
Pricing structure is split confusingly across standalone SuperGrok plans and separate X Premium bundles
Tight integration with Musk's other companies (X, SpaceX) raises questions about long-term platform independence
Grok's less-filtered personality and content moderation approach has drawn scrutiny compared to more conservative competitors

faqs.question: Is Grok free to use
faqs.answer: Yes. Grok has a free tier offering roughly 10 prompts every two hours on a lighter model, alongside paid SuperGrok subscriptions for higher usage limits and access to flagship models.

faqs.question: What is the difference between SuperGrok and X Premium+
faqs.answer: SuperGrok is a standalone subscription accessed directly through grok.com starting at 30 dollars per month, while X Premium+ at 40 dollars per month bundles Grok access together with ad-free browsing and other perks on the X platform.

faqs.question: Who owns Grok
faqs.answer: Grok is built by xAI, which Elon Musk founded in 2023, acquired X in an all-stock deal in March 2025, and was itself acquired by SpaceX in February 2026.

faqs.question: Can I use Grok on mobile
faqs.answer: Yes. Grok has dedicated iOS and Android apps, in addition to being accessible via the web at grok.com and within the X app.

faqs.question: Does Grok generate images and video
faqs.answer: Yes. Grok Imagine, launched in July 2025, lets users generate images and short videos from text prompts.

faqs.question: How much does the Grok API cost
faqs.answer: Grok API pricing varies by model; as of mid-2026 the flagship Grok 4.5 is priced at roughly 2 dollars per million input tokens and 6 dollars per million output tokens.
